data.title: SL Green projects $1.20-a-share FFO guidance increase, supported by $0.80 from One Vanderbilt accounting change
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SL Green (SLG) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Our economic occupancy… certainly did this quarter, up a remarkable 300 basis points as concessions continue to burn off and overall vacancy dwindles,” Marc Holliday (Chairman & CEO) said, adding, “We now know that we'll exceed our leasing goals again this year.”

  • Holliday highlighted SUMMIT momentum despite softer city tourism: “We enjoyed the highest attendance amongst all of our competitors… and introduced a number of new ticketed experiences that we expect will continue to drive revenue,” and added, “We are on track to open Paris next summer in 2027 and in Tokyo in 2030.”

  • “We are excited to be able to translate these successes into a significant upward FFO guidance revision of $1.20 a share, more than 26%, the vast majority of which is recurring,” Matthew Diliberto (Chief Financial Officer) said.

  • Diliberto detailed drivers: “revenues benefit from strong leasing… along with a conscious effort to accelerate GAAP revenue recognition… coupled with phenomenal expense containment,” plus One Vanderbilt: “The total of these 2 components contributes an additional $0.80 a share of FFO in '26, $0.35 of which we recorded in the second quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management framed the update as a major shift in 2026 earnings expectations: Holliday called it “our big guidance revision for this quarter,” and said, “We've executed a deliberate strategy to invest what was needed to move our occupancy back towards 95%, and we're now reaching a positive inflection point.”

  • Diliberto linked the 2026 revision to both real estate operations and fee income: the company expects “$0.20 a share of incremental FFO in 2026 from the real estate portfolio” and “an additional $0.20 a share of FFO” from “fee and other income,” with the remainder tied to One Vanderbilt’s incremental FFO mechanics.

  • On cash flow coverage timing, Diliberto reiterated: “'27 into '28, with the breakeven point in '28 is still the path that we are on at this point,” later clarifying “breakeven… as against coverage of your dividend.”

Financial Results

  • The company did not provide EPS or revenue figures in the transcript; management focused on FFO guidance revision mechanics and operating indicators.

  • Diliberto attributed part of 2026 uplift to execution already visible in Q2: “to drive $0.20 a share of incremental FFO in 2026 from the real estate portfolio… $0.10 of which we recognized in the second quarter.”

  • One Vanderbilt accounting treatment was positioned as a recurring earnings contributor: “This amortization component alone is approximately $21 million a year through the early part of 2031,” Diliberto said, adding, “Going forward, every dollar of cash distribution out of One Vanderbilt… is incremental FFO to us.”

  • On capital structure posture amid rates, Diliberto said, “We remain more like 90-10” fixed-to-floating and “we are maintaining a very cautious stance when it comes to rates.”

Q&A

  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: asked what drove strong mark-to-market leasing; Steven Durels (Executive VP and Director of Leasing & Real Property) said, “it's a broad-based improvement… Anything on Park Avenue, we've raised rents dramatically… 1185 6th, rents are up dramatically.”

  • Yulico, Scotiabank: asked about unlocking One Vanderbilt mark-to-market; Durels said, “there are opportunities… recapturing some of those spaces… and the rents will be up.”

  • Alexander Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: asked why recovery is so fast; Holliday cited “almost no addition to space,” companies planning long-term, and conversions: “you have an inventory that's actually dropping.”

  • Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: asked if office-to-resi pipeline could reverse; Holliday said economics are “closing the gap,” but “still tilts in favor of conversion.”

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: asked on refinancing/asset sales; Diliberto said SLG is “completed or in contract on 4 of the 11 deals,” expects “2 additional deals soon,” and said “the next one up in the queue is 245 Park.”

  • Sakwa, Evercore ISI: asked about 1515 Broadway after casino loss; Holliday said Paramount/Skydance developments “puts 1515 kind of squarely back in the mix,” and suggested more clarity “is next year.”

  • Thomas Catherwood, BTIG: asked embedded mark-to-market on COVID-era leases; Holliday gave “a broad range between 10% and 20%,” while Durels said many were short-term and “we don't have any big chunky expirations.”

  • Catherwood, BTIG: asked how the debt fund adapts as banks return; Harrison Sitomer (President & CIO) said deployments were “approximately $600 million,” and strategy is “financial engineering… build capital stacks to get to our yields.”

  • John Kim, BMO: asked what drove $0.20 operational uplift; Diliberto cited “renewals and early renewals,” faster turnover to “turn that earnings spigot on,” and expenses “ahead on expenses.”

  • Kim, BMO: asked how higher benchmarks affect refinancing; Sitomer cited CMBS market data and said SLG is “very confident in the execution” for 245 Park, while Diliberto emphasized hedging “well ahead of time.”

  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: asked pipeline update; Durels said “roughly 50% new, 50% renewal,” with “400,000 square feet… in active negotiation.”

  • Heck, Wells Fargo: asked why JV with Mori at 346 Madison now; Holliday said, “I love fully capitalized deals,” and SLG “reserved enough… to probably syndicate equity further down the line.”

  • Peter Abramowitz, Deutsche Bank: asked if FAD inflection timing changes; Diliberto said trajectory is “slightly ahead,” but still “'27 into '28.”

  • Abramowitz, Deutsche Bank: asked FIFA impact on SUMMIT; Holliday said there was “definitely a bump,” noted “daily ticket sales exceeding 400 and some-odd thousand a day,” and expects to “finish up right on our numbers.”

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: asked cap rates for upcoming dispositions; Holliday cited completed deals: “10 East 53rd Street… 5.7% cap rate” and “7 Dey… 5.0%.”

  • Paolone, JPMorgan: asked 750 Third and 346 Madison issues; Holliday said “no interruption” at 750 and on 346 access litigation: “we anticipate no adverse outcome and no adverse impact on timeline.”

  • Seth Bergey, Citi: asked buybacks vs debt paydown; Holliday said SLG used buybacks when “the price was not nearly reflective of the underlying value,” and may do more depending on “the landscape.”

  • Bergey, Citi: asked about an alternate/core FFO metric; Diliberto said, “No. I don't believe in violating what NAREIT says is FFO.”

  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: asked CapEx cadence and breakeven definition; Diliberto said CapEx is “a little back ended” and breakeven refers to “coverage of your dividend.”

  • Malhotra, Mizuho: asked cap rate ranges; Holliday said SLG assets are “decidedly between 5 and 6,” with “certain assets… sub-5.”

  • Malhotra, Mizuho: asked 2027 debt/swaps plan; Diliberto replied, “I plan to do that in December,” then “No.”

  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: asked commenced occupancy and 2027 NOI; Diliberto said SLG still expects “in excess of 10% same-store NOI, cash NOI growth in '27.”

  • Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: asked on certain alternative strategy assets; Holliday said they “need to be recapitalized,” contribute “little in the way of earnings,” and “we have no recourse to speak of.”

  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked how low free rent can go; Durels said renewals are “3 to 4 months” on a typical 5-year deal, and for new 10-year deals “get down to kind of the 10-month free rent.”

  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: asked why One Vanderbilt uplift discussed now and variability; Diliberto said it was vetted and “only after the end of the first quarter,” and future variability is driven by “distributions.”

  • Burrows, Goldman Sachs: asked SUMMIT expectations; Holliday said Q3 should show “some good numbers” and added, “I think we're going to have a very good second half of the year.”

  • Michael Lewis, Truist: asked AI tenant credit risk; Durels said AI exposure is “somewhere between 1% to 2% of the portfolio,” with key tenants “well capitalized.”

  • Lewis, Truist: asked about net effective rent growth transparency; Holliday said, “We don't track… net effective growth,” citing TI amortization complexity.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with repeated probing on sustainability and cash flow visibility; the most pressing exchange was Malhotra on 2027 planning, ending with Diliberto: “I plan to do that in December… No.”

  • Management’s tone was positive and assertive in prepared remarks (Holliday: “I remain very optimistic”), while in Q&A it was confident on leasing/capital markets but occasionally firm or limiting on disclosure (Diliberto: “No.”).

  • Versus Q1, the tone shifted from “excellent quarter” and leasing records to a stronger emphasis on a “significant upward” guidance change and One Vanderbilt’s incremental FFO mechanics, with more pointed analyst questions on the quality/visibility of earnings.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The largest change from Q1 to Q2 was the explicit “significant upward FFO guidance revision of $1.20 a share,” including “an additional $0.80 a share” tied to One Vanderbilt accounting mechanics; Q1 focused on leasing volume records and business-plan progress without a comparable guidance step-up.

  • Strategic emphasis remained consistent on Midtown scarcity and conversions, but Q2 added more detail on investment sales execution status (“4 of the 11 deals”) and hedging posture (“more like 90-10” fixed-to-floating).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q1 cadence/pipeline composition and dividend/FAD framing to Q2 scrutiny of the One Vanderbilt uplift’s variability, capital markets timing, and the company’s willingness to outline a 2027 plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rate and benchmark volatility remained a key issue; Sitomer said, “the benchmark… is not cooperating with us,” while management’s mitigation was hedging and maintaining a heavily fixed profile.

  • Development execution and neighbor-related litigation surfaced; Holliday described 346 Madison access litigation as “fairly… routine,” and DeWitt detailed “numerous layers of oversight” for 750 Third structural controls.

  • Tourism softness was acknowledged as a headwind; Holliday said, “foreign tourism particularly has been substandard,” while pointing to variable expense management and new ticketed experiences at SUMMIT.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a step-change in 2026 earnings expectations, led by a $1.20-per-share upward FFO guidance revision that includes a $0.80-per-share recurring contribution tied to One Vanderbilt’s accounting treatment, while reiterating strengthening Manhattan leasing conditions, continued rent appreciation across key Midtown corridors, and ongoing capital recycling and financing execution under a hedged balance sheet posture.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan's manufacturing PMI beats forecasts at 54.7 in July; composite PMI hits highest level since February at 53.1
Australia's private sector activity accelerates in July as services & factory orders rebound
data.description:
Private sector activity in Australia accelerated in July 2026 to its highest level since January, propelled by a strong rebound in services activity and the first rise in new manufacturing orders in five months.

The S&P Global Australia Manufacturing PMI inched up to 51.7 from 51.5 in the prior month, marking a fourth consecutive month of expansion. Factory growth was underpinned by a return to new order growth, driven by stronger domestic demand and new client wins, while manufacturing employment expanded at its fastest pace since April.

The S&P Global Flash Australia Services PMI Business Activity Index increased to 53.0 in July 2026 from 50.5 in June, signaling a sharp pickup in services-sector expansion to a six-month high.

The S&P Global Flash Australia Composite PMI climbed to 52.6 in July from 50.4 in June, marking the strongest overall growth rate since January.

Despite the upbeat economic survey data, Australian financial markets pulled back around midday, the S&P/ASX 200 Index dipped 26 points, or 0.3%, to 8,813, reversing gains from the previous three sessions. The Australian dollar traded below $0.698 and remained on track for its first weekly decline in a month.

data.title: Galaxy Digital prices $3.5B senior secured notes offering
data.description:
• Galaxy Digital (GLXY) priced a $3.507B private offering of 9.875% senior secured notes due 2031 through its indirect subsidiary, Galaxy Helios Data Centers II LLC.
  • The notes will pay 9.875% annual interest, payable semiannually beginning February 1, 2027, and will mature on August 1, 2031.
  • Net proceeds will fund part of the development and construction of a Texas AI data center project and debt service reserves.
  • The project includes two buildings with eight data halls, providing 400 MW of utility capacity and 260 MW of critical IT capacity on a 260-acre site in Dickens County, Texas.
  • The offering is expected to close on July 28, 2026.

data.title: Allianz to acquire HSBC’s Singapore insurance unit for $2.09B
data.description:
• Allianz Group has agreed to acquire HSBC’s (HSBC) Singapore life insurance unit for 2.7 billion Singapore dollars ($2.09 billion), expanding the company’s footprint in Asia’s life and health insurance market.
  • The German insurer expects to generate a double-digit return on investment in the medium-term, and the transaction is expected to close in the first half of 2027.
  • As part of the deal, Allianz will also enter a 15-year exclusive distribution partnership with HSBC Singapore (HSBC) to strengthen its ties in the region.
  • Allianz aims “to support more individuals and communities even more comprehensively, with a broader product portfolio that helps protect and plan for what matters most to them,” said Renate Wagner, a member of the board of management. 
  • HSBC Life Singapore generated an operating profit of 80 million euros ($91 million) in 2025.

data.title: Japan's core CPI rebounds to 1.6% in June; annual inflation rises to 1.7%
MaxsMaking gets delisting notice from Nasdaq
data.description:
• MaxsMaking (MAMK) received a Nasdaq staff delisting determination after an SEC temporary trading suspension on Nov. 17, 2025, after which Nasdaq halted trading in MAMK shares on Dec. 2, 2025.
  • Nasdaq said the stock was susceptible to third-party price manipulation, indicating insufficient liquidity to support fair and orderly markets.
  • The exchange said delisting is consistent with investor protection and the public interest.
  • MaxsMaking (MAMK) plans to request a hearing before an independent Nasdaq Hearings Panel.

data.title: Avidia Bancorp, Inc. GAAP EPS of $0.39, revenue of $29.86M
data.description:
• Avidia Bancorp, Inc. press release (AVBC): Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.39.
  • Revenue of $29.86M.

  • The period-end allowance for credit losses was $23.9 million in the second quarter of 2026 compared to $22.8 million at the prior quarter-end. The ratio of the allowance to total loans increased to 1.06% from 1.00% for these dates.

    • Provision expense for credit losses decreased $197 thousand to $892 thousand.
    • The Company recorded a benefit to the allowance of $420 thousand for net recoveries in the second quarter, compared to net charge-offs of $116 thousand recorded in the prior quarter.
    • Non-accruing loans were $16.9 million, or 0.75% of total loans, at period-end, compared to $13.6 million, or 0.60% of total loans, at the prior quarter-end, primarily due to an increase in non-accruing commercial & industrial loans.

data.title: Scholastic projects 2%-4% revenue growth and $135M-$145M adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2027, following 25% dividend increase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholastic Corporation (SCHL) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Fiscal 2026 was an important year for Scholastic. It demonstrated the earnings power of the more focused company we've been building" (President, CEO & Director Peter Warwick), while also emphasizing capital actions: "The sale-leaseback transactions unlocked over $400 million in net proceeds" and "During the year, we returned over $285 million to shareholders."

  • On operating drivers, Warwick highlighted the mix of strengths and headwinds: "Book Fairs continued to perform well. Entertainment returned to growth, and education showed improving trends even as headwinds continued."

  • Glover summarized the quarter and full-year topline: "Revenues decreased 6% to $476.1 million in the fourth quarter and 3% to $1.6 billion in the full fiscal year" (CFO & Executive Vice President Haji Glover).

  • Glover tied profitability and EPS to portfolio performance and capital actions: "Adjusted net income was $45.9 million in the fourth quarter" and "On a per diluted share basis, adjusted earnings were $2.19 compared to $0.87 last year," adding that "fewer shares outstanding following share repurchase activity also benefited adjusted earnings per share."

Outlook

  • "For fiscal 2027, we expect revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4% compared to fiscal 2026 reported revenue and adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million" (CFO Glover).

  • On quarterly cadence, Glover said, "we expect first quarter revenues to be slightly down versus prior year period" and "Year-over-year revenue growth is expected to begin in the second quarter and continue through the balance of the year," while also flagging profitability seasonality: "We also expect a seasonal operating loss modestly greater than the prior year period, primarily reflecting the sale leaseback impact."

  • On cash generation, Glover guided: "Our outlook for free cash flow in fiscal 2027 is approximately $35 million to $40 million."

Financial Results

  • Glover reported profitability metrics and sale-leaseback comparability framing: "Adjusted EBITDA was $84.7 million in the fourth quarter compared to $91.2 million in the prior year period" and "On the same comparable basis, fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA increased $1 million."

  • In Children’s Book Publishing and Distribution, Glover said, "revenues for the fourth quarter decreased 4% to $276.3 million," while Book Fairs grew: "Book Fairs revenues increased 5% in the fourth quarter to $186.6 million" and "over 103,000 case and shippable fairs held during the fiscal year."

  • Education remained pressured: "fourth quarter revenues were $109.2 million, down 13%" and "full year revenues were $267.6 million, a decrease of 14%" (CFO Glover), with the driver stated as "ongoing funding volatility."

  • Entertainment showed sharp Q4 growth: "fourth quarter revenues increased 42% to $21 million" and "Segment adjusted operating income improved $2.9 million to $0.8 million in the fourth quarter compared to a loss of $2.1 million a year ago" (CFO Glover).

Q&A

  • Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "That 3% decline came in a little bit under the expectations and the guidance for the year. Just what were the key variables there that drove that impact?" President Warwick: "the 2 main factors were" the prior-year comp from "Sunrise on the Reaping" and that "we also didn't get as much revenue from the education segment as we were expecting."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "Was that really driven by higher fair count? Or did more of that growth come from better economics and revenue per fair?" President Warwick: "It was primarily driven by the number of fairs" and "we also saw a modest increase in revenue per fair as well," adding "much more adoption of e-wallets" and "a really good end of the year in our sponsored fairs."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "do you see improved unit economics" and "what's the expectation for total fair count" in fiscal 2027? President Warwick: "we expect fair count to increase in fiscal 2027" and "We'll expect to see revenue per fair grow maybe" similarly to fiscal 2026.

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "What's the outlook" for Education in fiscal 2027 and what supports stabilization? President Warwick: "we have a stronger product portfolio" and "a new Chief Revenue Officer, who joined us during the fourth quarter," adding "we feel pretty confident" that performance improves "over the full course of the year."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "What kind of maybe revenue run rate" is needed for sustained Entertainment profitability? President Warwick: "we've got very strong visibility" and "we're already at over 100% of last year's revenues" in contracted revenues.

  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "how would you assess the health of your consumer?" President Warwick: "we are feeling pretty good" citing "the strength of our Publishing" and "Book Fair, bookings are good."

  • Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "are you assuming revenue can grow year-on-year in 3Q, 4Q" for Education? President Warwick: "the assumption that you're making... is correct" and "you'll be able to see that in the second half of the year."

  • Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "fund buybacks with cash" or use the revolver? CFO Glover: "we are strong on cash" and "we'll pull from both sources if needed," adding "we feel very confident we have enough cash to fund our $183 million of potential opportunity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on revenue/education visibility, using framing such as "under the expectations" and probing for quantification: "maybe quantify what that ultimately looks like" (Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confidence-leaning on core channels and FY27 execution, using language including "we feel pretty confident" (President Warwick) and "we've got very strong visibility" (President Warwick), while also narrowing assumptions: "Our outlook does not currently assume a material impact from additional tariff actions" (CFO Glover).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s heavier balance-sheet/tender-offer emphasis ("the Board has authorized a $300 million share repurchase authorization" (President Warwick, Q3)), Q4 language shifted toward operating execution: "Fiscal 2027 is an execution year" (President Warwick).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Capital return messaging evolved from announcing the tender framework in Q3 ("a $200 million modified Dutch Auction tender offer" (President Warwick, Q3)) to reporting outcomes in Q4: "we returned over $285 million to shareholders" and "repurchase of approximately 7.3 million shares... for $268.6 million" (CFO Glover, Q4).

  • Guidance posture shifted from Q3 reaffirmation ("we're reaffirming our fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow guidance" (President Warwick, Q3)) to Q4 issuing FY27 targets: "revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4%" and "adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million" (CFO Glover, Q4).

  • Education narrative moved from Q3 deceleration optimism ("Revenues were down 2%" (President Warwick, Q3)) to Q4 acknowledging larger declines alongside a second-half trend: "the rate of decline decelerated in the second half of fiscal 2026" (CFO Glover, Q4).

Risks and Concerns

  • Funding volatility in Education remained the central operational risk: "education continues to operate in a pressured purchasing environment" (President Warwick) and "school and district funding conditions remain volatile" (CFO Glover).

  • Trade and International comparisons were called out as headwinds tied to prior-year timing: "a challenging comparison with the prior year fourth quarter publishing schedule" (CFO Glover).

  • Tariff assumptions were bounded but still a watch item: "Our outlook reflects current tariff rates" and "Our outlook does not currently assume a material impact from additional tariff actions" (CFO Glover).

Final Takeaway

Management framed fiscal 2026 as the proof point of a more focused model and a reshaped balance sheet, highlighted by sale-leaseback proceeds and accelerated shareholder returns, while positioning fiscal 2027 as an execution year with guided revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4%, adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million, and free cash flow of approximately $35 million to $40 million, led by continued Book Fairs momentum, a stronger trade pipeline tied to major franchises, improving Entertainment visibility, and an Education plan centered on stabilization amid ongoing funding volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Headwater Exploration Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49, revenue of $237.9M
data.description:
• Headwater Exploration press release (HWX:CA): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49.
  • Revenue of $237.9M.

  • The Board of Directors of Headwater has declared a quarterly cash dividend to shareholders of $0.12 per common share payable on October 15, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on September 30, 2026. This dividend is an eligible dividend for the purposes of the Income Tax Act (Canada).
